title: Lawn Signs

jokes.text: Moira bursts in declaring she's been 'hijacked' before immediately pivoting to asking for tea
jokes.setup: Moira dramatically declares her campaign has been hijacked, building urgency
jokes.punchline: She immediately interrupts her own crisis to ask 'I need some tea. Twyla?' — the urgency evaporates into entitled routine
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Moira: the world-ending crisis is immediately subordinated to creature comforts. The tea request mid-sentence deflates the drama in a way only this character could. High character integration — this is unmistakably Moira's comic register.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's just missing lawn signs

jokes.text: 'Maybe people were mowing their lawns.' / 'Well, it's probably just kids.'
jokes.setup: Moira is in full dramatic crisis mode about her missing signs
jokes.punchline: The people around her offer mundane, deflating explanations — lawnmowers, kids — puncturing her theatrics
jokes.explanation: Functional straight-man comedy — the grounded responses contrast nicely with Moira's escalating panic. Works but is not exceptional; standard sitcom deflation beat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The signs have disappeared. Someone's taken them. It's a coordinated effort.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, the lawn service was here yesterday.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What does that have to do with anything?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: They mowed over them.

jokes.text: 'What if there's a more sinister explanation?' / 'Oh, like the wind.'
jokes.setup: Moira teases a 'sinister explanation' for the missing signs
jokes.punchline: John deadpans 'Oh, like the wind' — matching her dramatic register but immediately undermining it with the most banal possible answer
jokes.explanation: John's dry delivery here is excellent — he plays along with Moira's gravitas only to substitute 'the wind' as his sinister culprit. Economy is nearly perfect: three words does maximum comic damage. Solid professional comedy writing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A sinister explanation? For missing signs?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, what if it's the wind?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The wind is not sinister, Mother.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You clearly haven't been paying attention to the wind.

jokes.text: Moira's monologue: 'I was wondering if there's someone who's thinking, Oh, this woman may not have many signs, but she has myriad qualities worth voting for. I must stop her!'
jokes.setup: The group is trying to rationally explain the missing signs
jokes.punchline: Moira constructs an elaborate paranoid fantasy in which her very excellence is so threatening that someone stole signs to suppress her — complete with internal monologue of her imagined enemy
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira self-aggrandizement: the leap from 'signs are missing' to 'someone fears my myriad qualities' is both narcissistic and completely in-character. The word 'myriad' lands as a comic choice — it's too elevated for the situation. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I imagine they're thinking: 'I'll steal this sign, yes. No one will know it was me. I'll take it home, and I'll mount it above my mantelpiece, and when guests arrive, they'll say, "Where did you get that magnificent sign?" And I'll say, "Oh, I stole it from a woman in Schitt's Creek." And they'll say, "You're very brave and also very cool." And then I'll die, and my children will inherit the sign, and they'll tell their children, and it will become a family heirloom, and centuries from now, archeologists will unearth it and say, "This sign was very important to a very stupid person."'

jokes.text: 'So you think the signs were stolen?' / 'Stolen? Ew! Is that what you think happened?'
jokes.setup: John simply repeats back what Moira has been implying throughout
jokes.punchline: Moira recoils at the word 'stolen' as if she hadn't just been describing exactly that — her selective outrage is the joke
jokes.explanation: Moira's 'Ew!' to the word 'stolen' — as if the vocabulary is more offensive than the concept — is a nice character-specific comic beat. Her denial of her own implication is very Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Stolen? I don't appreciate the implication. Our family would never steal a sign. We would simply... relocate it to a place where it could be better appreciated.

jokes.text: Moira's talking head: 'Someone is imitated enough into thinking that I might be affecting this town's long-established voting patterns.'
jokes.setup: Moira delivers a confessional about finding the silver lining in the sign theft
jokes.punchline: She mispronounces 'intimidated' as 'imitated' — either a malapropism or a character quirk — while simultaneously constructing the most self-flattering possible interpretation of a minor local election
jokes.explanation: The combination of the Moira-ism ('imitated' for 'intimidated') and the grandiose framing of a small-town council race as threatening 'long-established voting patterns' is very Schitt's Creek. The malapropism adds a layer — it's ambiguous whether it's intentional character voice or genuine error, which is itself funny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose there is a silver lining. At least they imitated me enough to steal my sign.

jokes.text: Roland reveals he has plastered the town with Jocelyn signs, and John calls it 'overkill a week ago'
jokes.setup: Roland cheerfully announces he's 'a step ahead' on signage
jokes.punchline: John reveals it's already been overkill for a week — the visual of a town drowning in Jocelyn signs is established
jokes.explanation: A solid world-building gag — Roland's mayoral excess with campaign signs fits his character perfectly. The phrase 'overkill a week ago' does the work efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh yes, because nothing says 'fair playing field' like Jocelyn's billboards plastered all over town.

jokes.text: Roland's dawning realization: 'Wait. Are you saying that I... had something...' — trailing off guiltily
jokes.setup: John mentions Moira's signs all disappeared; Roland was just bragging about his own sign dominance
jokes.punchline: Roland's guilt-laden, halting sentence — 'Are you saying that I... had something...' — is funnier than any completed accusation would be
jokes.explanation: The unfinished sentence is the joke — Roland's inability to complete the incriminating thought reads as guilt while maintaining plausible deniability. The structure (trailing off mid-sentence) is where the craft lives.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, little lady, I think what you're trying to say is that you appreciate a man who knows how to advertise himself.

jokes.text: John and Roland's circular threats: 'if I find out you're accusing me of something I didn't do, then I'm going to accuse you of making false accusations'
jokes.setup: John accuses Roland (without quite accusing him); Roland denies (while acting guilty)
jokes.punchline: Roland's counter-threat is pure logical nonsense — he'll accuse John of making false accusations as punishment — a threat so circular it loops back around to funny
jokes.explanation: The escalating mutual threats where neither man can quite say what he means is excellent two-hander comedy. Roland's threat to counter-accuse John of making false accusations is perfectly nonsensical in a Roland-specific way.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: She had signs?

jokes.text: Oh. Moira had lawn signs? / She had a few. / Now she has none.
jokes.setup: Roland seems genuinely surprised Moira had signs
jokes.punchline: John's dry 'She had a few. Now she has none.' economically indicts Roland while playing on the contrast of Jocelyn's 200 vs. Moira's zero
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy — three short sentences do tremendous work: establishing the sign disparity, implying Roland's guilt, and playing John's dry wit perfectly. The rhythm of 'She had a few. Now she has none.' is almost musical in its precision.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know what? I don't appreciate the insinuation. I'm a businessman, not a sign thief. But if you keep it up, I'll steal your sign just to prove a point.

jokes.text: The threats spiral: 'I don't wanna tell you how I respond to threats.' / 'And I don't wanna tell you how I respond to illegal political activity.'
jokes.setup: The circular argument about accusations has reached a standoff
jokes.punchline: Both men make vague, escalating non-threats — the menace is entirely performative since neither will specify consequences
jokes.explanation: The structure here is excellent — two mirror-image non-threats, each deliberately incomplete. 'I don't wanna tell you how I respond to threats' as a threat is genuinely clever. The parallel structure amplifies the absurdity. Strong economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So what you're saying is that you're upset with me because I'm upset with you about you being upset with me?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, no, I'm upset because—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Because I'm upset? Or because you're upset that I'm upset about your being upset?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Johnny, I don't—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Because if it's the former, then you're being hypocritical. If it's the latter, then I have every right to be upset about your upset, which makes your upset about my upset completely invalid.

jokes.text: After the standoff, a long beat, then Roland says simply: 'Maybe.'
jokes.setup: Both men have just declared 'Fine, I won't' in mutual standoff
jokes.punchline: 'Maybe' — Roland undercuts the finality of the standoff with a single word implying everything is still on the table
jokes.explanation: One word doing maximum work. After an elaborate standoff of non-threats and 'Fine, I won't' declarations, 'Maybe' collapses the entire structure and reveals Roland has no intention of backing down. Perfect economy (10/10 — literally one word) and perfect structural placement as the button. This is among the episode's best constructed jokes. The pause before it makes it land harder. Justifies the 8+ score.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not going to tell anyone.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I might tell Moira. And David. And possibly Alexis. But that's it.

jokes.text: Okay, fine, then don't. / Fine, I won't. [beat] Maybe.
jokes.setup: The men have resolved to say nothing
jokes.punchline: A beat of silence, then Roland's 'Maybe' reopens the whole standoff with one word
jokes.explanation: The single word 'Maybe' after the apparent resolution is beautifully economical. It's the perfect Roland move — he can't help himself — and the beat before it makes it land harder. Structure is very clean: setup (the resolution), beat (silence), punchline (one word that undoes everything). Among the better jokes in the episode for pure craft.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: David, I'm so sorry, but I have to let you go. It's just not working out.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? Why? I thought things were going well.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: They were, but I've decided to make some changes. And honestly, letting you go was my best idea yet.

jokes.text: John's reaction to the 'Maybe': 'Oh dear God. Yes.'
jokes.setup: Roland's 'Maybe' has just punctured the standoff
jokes.punchline: John's 'Oh dear God. Yes.' — resigned exhaustion combined with implied agreement — is a perfect straight-man button to the scene
jokes.explanation: John's three-word response captures his weary capitulation to Roland's chaos perfectly. The 'Yes' after 'Oh dear God' implies agreement with whatever unspoken arrangement they've reached — the specificity of his defeat is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: I'm so broke, I had to cancel my membership to the Costco. And the gym. And apparently I was still paying for a hot yoga studio in Portland.

jokes.text: The scene cuts to what appears to be a phone call about finances: 'Well, take it out of my savings account' / 'Can you put some of the air miles towards the electricity bill?'
jokes.setup: The Rose family's financial ruin is an ongoing thread
jokes.punchline: John is trying to pay electricity with air miles — the gap between their former wealth and present desperation is played as absurdist dark humor
jokes.explanation: The air miles gag captures the Rose family's inability to recalibrate from wealth to poverty — they still think in luxury travel currency while struggling with utilities. Solid fish-out-of-water beat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can believe it. It's a terrible name. I've never understood why anyone would open a store called Blouse Barn. It sounds like a place where blouses go to die.

jokes.text: David reacts to Wendy's store signage: 'Okay, this signage is not really on brand.'
jokes.setup: David is working as a brand manager at a store called 'Blouse Barn'
jokes.punchline: His deadpan brand-manager critique in the context of a small-town clothing store called 'Blouse Barn' is self-aware comedy — the pretension of the critique vs. the reality of the store
jokes.explanation: David's urbane brand-speak applied to 'Blouse Barn' is a consistent source of comedy this episode. The gap between his vocabulary and the setting does the work.

dialogue.speaker: David Rose
dialogue.line: I prefer to think of it as a gesture.

dialogue.speaker: Moira Rose
dialogue.line: A gesture? David, it's a legal settlement.

jokes.text: Wendy: 'I still can't believe we even sold one [poncho].'
jokes.setup: David is arguing his changes have helped the store by citing poncho sales
jokes.punchline: Wendy's aside — 'I still can't believe we even sold one' — undercuts his whole argument while being a credible business concern
jokes.explanation: A brief but well-placed line — her genuine disbelief at the poncho sale inadvertently makes David's case while simultaneously undermining it. Efficient comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: At least it's not Arby's.

jokes.text: Wendy describes her financial situation: 'down to a Costco card and a zoo membership'
jokes.setup: Wendy is explaining why she has to let David go due to financial trouble
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'a Costco card and a zoo membership' as the last remnants of her lifestyle — both utterly mundane but weirdly specific — makes the poverty funny rather than sad
jokes.explanation: The specificity of these two items is the craft: a Costco card (bulk bargain shopping) and a zoo membership (wholesome leisure) paint a vivid picture of someone trying to hold onto normalcy while collapsing financially. The pairing is unexpected and funny. Quotable line — the kind that sticks. Justifies 8+ on originality: the specific items chosen do real comedic work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have trouble with Australians. They're all just so... outdoorsy and confident. It's deeply unsettling.

jokes.text: Wendy reveals the Blouse Barn Australia situation — they'll pay $10,000 for the name. David: 'That there's another store called Blouse Barn? No.'
jokes.setup: Wendy excitedly shares good news: a Blouse Barn in Australia will pay her to use the name
jokes.punchline: David misreads her 'can you believe it?' as being about the name itself — expressing disbelief that 'Blouse Barn' is anyone else's brand choice
jokes.explanation: David's misreading of 'Can you believe it?' — assuming it refers to the absurdity of anyone else wanting the name 'Blouse Barn' rather than the business deal — is both character-perfect (his contempt for the name) and structurally elegant (misdirection on the antecedent of 'it'). The line 'That there's another store called Blouse Barn? No.' is sharp, economical, and quotable. Solid 8: comparable to a well-crafted comeback that reveals character. Justifies the score.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know, I've learned most of my business acumen from watching television.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Specifically, from watching people fail spectacularly on reality TV.

jokes.text: Wendy's inadvertent admission: 'Apparently, I have been using the name without their permission.'
jokes.setup: Wendy has been framing the $10,000 as a goodwill 'gesture' from Australia
jokes.punchline: The truth emerges naturally — it's not a gesture, it's a settlement. The understated delivery of 'apparently' makes it funnier.
jokes.explanation: The word 'apparently' does the comic heavy lifting — Wendy's obliviousness to her own legal situation is gently funny. The reveal reframes the entire conversation retroactively.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have a question.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The answer is no.

jokes.text: David: 'And to think I almost called this place Blouse House.'
jokes.setup: David is suspicious about the Australia deal
jokes.punchline: Wendy non-sequiturs with relief that she didn't call it 'Blouse House' — which is equally terrible, making her relief absurd
jokes.explanation: 'Blouse House' as the dodged bullet is funny because it's essentially just as bad as 'Blouse Barn.' Her relief is misplaced, which is the joke. Brief but well-delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's been quite fun, actually. Going after these trademark violators, sending cease and desist letters. It's like a fun shakedown.

jokes.text: David: 'Something seems really sketchy about this, and I'm not just saying that because I have a hard time with Australians. Lotta drunks.'
jokes.setup: David is expressing skepticism about the Australian company's deal
jokes.punchline: He clarifies his suspicion isn't about Australians per se — then immediately confirms exactly that prejudice with 'Lotta drunks'
jokes.explanation: The structure is excellent: David pre-emptively denies a prejudice, then immediately confirms it in two words. 'Lotta drunks' as a self-aware follow-through on his own denial is character-perfect and quotable. The economy is superb — the joke sets up and pays off in one breath. David's particular brand of opinionated absurdity is unmistakable. Justifies 8+: the structure (deny then confirm) is genuinely clever, not just a stereotype joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, so hypothetically, you owned Blouse Barn.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, first of all, I would change the name.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Of course you would.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's pedestrian. And the inventory system is a nightmare. You'd need to completely overhaul the supply chain, modernize the point-of-sale software, and frankly, the demographic you're targeting is aging out of the market.

jokes.text: If there's one thing I've learned from 'The Good Wife', it's never accept a first offer.
jokes.setup: David is advising Wendy on the negotiation
jokes.punchline: David's legal/negotiation knowledge comes from a TV drama, and he cites it with complete confidence as professional wisdom
jokes.explanation: A perfectly David moment — citing a TV legal drama as a source of professional wisdom with complete earnestness. The joke is his confidence. 'The Good Wife' is a specific and funny choice. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Ten thousand dollars for the name 'Blouse Barn'? That's... that's a lot of money.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, that's what I'm asking.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know what? Done. I'll take it.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Really? Just like that?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes. The name is so terrible, I'm not going to get a better deal anywhere else.

jokes.text: John's first response to David entering: 'Yes, your cologne is too strong.'
jokes.setup: David says 'I have a question'
jokes.punchline: John preemptively answers a question David didn't ask — about David's cologne — before David can speak, implying this is a frequent complaint
jokes.explanation: A great misdirection — 'I have a question' sets us up for a business query; John's preemptive 'your cologne is too strong' tells us this has been said before and the question is always about the cologne. Strong structure (misdirection + implied history) and economy. Very John-David dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Were you fired?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I prefer the term 'professionally released.'

jokes.text: David: 'I just put it on and it needs to breathe a bit, so...'
jokes.setup: John has just said David's cologne is too strong
jokes.punchline: David's defensive explanation — that the cologne 'needs to breathe' — is both a real thing people say about perfume and a ridiculous excuse for an overpowering fragrance
jokes.explanation: David's defense uses real perfume terminology ('needs to breathe') applied to a social complaint in a way that's both plausible and obviously insufficient. His defensiveness without acknowledgment of the problem is very David.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, David, sometimes in life you just have to take a risk. Put yourself out there.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you, that's... that's very helpful, Dad.

jokes.text: John: 'I remember a Rosie's Video in Sao Paolo. That was a fun shakedown.'
jokes.setup: David asks if anyone ever asked John to stop using the Rose Video name
jokes.punchline: John's fond, almost nostalgic recall of legally threatening a small competitor in Brazil — called a 'fun shakedown' — reveals the casual corporate ruthlessness behind the Rose fortune
jokes.explanation: John cheerfully describing bullying a small business as 'a fun shakedown' is dark comedy that also deepens character — we see how the Roses made their money and why they don't register it as morally complex. 'Fun shakedown' as a phrase is specific and memorable. High character integration: this is exactly the kind of detail that makes John dimensional.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, my grandmother had a similar experience. A bird flew into her window and died, and she said it was a sign that a ghost was trying to get into the house. So she boarded up all the windows and doors, and she lived in that house for three years without leaving. When they finally found her, she'd forgotten what the outside world looked like, and she spent the rest of her life thinking she was living in a log cabin in the woods. She wasn't. It was a split-level in Kitchener.

jokes.text: John: 'First of all, I'd never get into the clothing business, 'cause the margins are terrible...'
jokes.setup: David asks John to hypothetically pretend he owns Blouse Barn
jokes.punchline: John immediately breaks the hypothetical to critique clothing retail margins — his businessman brain can't even play pretend without a profit assessment
jokes.explanation: John's inability to engage with a hypothetical without first vetting it as a business proposition is a great character beat. The compulsive pivot to margins is funny precisely because it's so John.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh, I'd love to help with your campaign signs, Moira, but I actually can't because I'm supporting Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, well that's... that's very honest of you.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I want to win.

jokes.text: John: '$10,000 to stop using the name Blouse Barn? I would take that in a second. It's a terrible name.'
jokes.setup: David is trying to get John's advice on whether Wendy should accept the deal
jokes.punchline: John would accept immediately — not on business grounds but because the name is terrible. He then pivots to defending 'Rose Video' aesthetics.
jokes.explanation: John's blunt 'it's a terrible name' is a satisfying puncture of David's strategic framing. The Rose Video brand defense that follows ('said what it was, the logo looked great on a golf shirt') is a nice character-specific add.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm actually really good under pressure. Like, way better than David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not true.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It is! Remember when I accidentally started an international incident at that gala in Monaco? I handled that so well. The ambassador said I was 'composed.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You caused an international incident.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Exactly. And I was calm the whole time.

jokes.text: John defends Rose Video: 'Now at least with Rose Video, it said what it was, and the logo looked great on a golf shirt.'
jokes.setup: John is disparaging the Blouse Barn name while comparing it to his own brand
jokes.punchline: His defense of Rose Video culminates in 'the logo looked great on a golf shirt' — a very specific, very John-ish metric for brand quality
jokes.explanation: The golf shirt metric is specific to John's WASP-adjacent business sensibility. It's an unexpected standard and the specificity earns the laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so when you go into this negotiation, they're going to try to throw you off. They're going to say things that are designed to make you emotional or defensive.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So I'm going to practice with you. I'm going to say something really embarrassing, and you just have to stay calm and composed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Fine. Go ahead.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You still sleep with a humidifier that makes whale sounds.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, that's not—how did you even—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Stay composed!

jokes.text: David: 'She can't even afford me anymore, so...' — revealing to John he was fired
jokes.setup: John asks if Wendy can afford a legal fight; David says she can't
jokes.punchline: David casually reveals he's already been fired in the same sentence — treating his own termination as a minor supporting data point
jokes.explanation: David's buried lede — treating his firing as a parenthetical while focusing on Wendy's situation — is both funny and revealing. The 'so...' trailing off is the perfect delivery beat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't have a Cosmo Girl subscription.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, your eye does that thing where it twitches when you lie. You literally can't help yourself.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My eye does not twitch.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, well, if you ever need to lie about anything, just keep your eye very still, and don't move your mouth, and try not to move any other part of your body. Also, speak in a completely different voice.

jokes.text: David: 'Technically, I would say I'm in a transition phase at the moment.'
jokes.setup: John says 'Wait a minute. You were fired?'
jokes.punchline: David's PR-speak reframe of being fired as a 'transition phase' is perfect David Rose self-preservation language
jokes.explanation: A functional character beat — the euphemism is expected but delivered with enough David-specific dignity to work. Not original in concept but well-integrated.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Her own clothes aren't intimidating enough

jokes.text: John immediately pivots to updating David's resume and 'getting him back into the workforce'
jokes.setup: John has just heard David was fired from a small-town blouse shop
jokes.punchline: John's instant pivot to resume-updating and workforce reintegration — treating a minimum-wage-equivalent job like a C-suite crisis — reveals both his paternal instinct and his total disconnect from their reality
jokes.explanation: John's corporate-world instincts applied to David's situation at Blouse Barn is a gentle fish-out-of-water joke. Works as character comedy but isn't exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, Wendy, you just have to trust me. I've been in way more high-pressure situations than this. I once had to negotiate the seating arrangement at a royal wedding in Monaco, and let me tell you, one wrong move and people literally could have died.

jokes.text: Um, it was entirely unhelpful, but thank you nevertheless.
jokes.setup: John's advice has been mostly off-topic and unhelpful
jokes.punchline: David's polite but devastatingly honest farewell — 'entirely unhelpful, but thank you nevertheless' — is a perfectly calibrated dismissal
jokes.explanation: 'Entirely unhelpful, but thank you nevertheless' is one of the episode's most quotable lines. The structure — acknowledging the thanks while negating it — is very David, and the 'nevertheless' adds an archaic politeness that makes it funnier. High economy, strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm not actually a lawyer. I just dress like one.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, I got these clothes at a thrift store and people just started treating me differently. It's actually been really helpful.

jokes.text: John says 'Well... anytime, son.' after David has explicitly said his advice was 'entirely unhelpful'
jokes.setup: David thanks John but clarifies the advice was 'entirely unhelpful'
jokes.punchline: John responds with warm sincerity — 'Well... anytime, son' — as if the unhelpfulness is irrelevant to his willingness to help again
jokes.explanation: The warmth of 'anytime, son' in response to explicit criticism is both funny and emotionally resonant — a hallmark of Schitt's Creek's best comedy. The pause before 'anytime' makes it land. Strong economy: three words that carry affection, obliviousness, and comedy simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Alexis Rose with Schitt's Creek Legal Associates.

jokes.text: Choir member: 'Two days ago, we had someone break our window. They didn't take anything, but they left a dead bird inside.'
jokes.setup: The choir is discussing the culture of fear created by Moira's missing signs
jokes.punchline: The escalating crime stories culminate in a break-in where the perpetrator left a dead bird — a surreal, specific detail that is funnier than any conventional crime would be
jokes.explanation: The dead bird is genuinely strange and funny — the specificity ('didn't take anything, but left a dead bird') suggests a sinister motive we can't quite parse, which makes it funnier. Strong originality. The detail feels real to small-town bizarre crime.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, we were going to use them, but then we realized the Rose Apothecary is more of a word-of-mouth business.

jokes.text: Another choir member: 'That's kind of like my aunt. She has a ghost in her house that keeps leaving dimes everywhere.'
jokes.setup: A choir member has just shared a disturbing crime story about a dead bird
jokes.punchline: Another member equates this with her aunt's ghost that leaves dimes — a non-sequitur equivalency that reveals the bizarre logic of small-town Schitt's Creek residents
jokes.explanation: The ghost leaving dimes is a beautifully specific absurdist image — dimes in particular, not coins, not money, dimes — and the casual equivalence with a break-in and dead bird reflects the show's skill at writing small-town eccentrics. Highly quotable and original.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: There are signs, David. Signs everywhere. In the garbage. Little messages.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, those are just... labels.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You're not seeing the bigger picture. It's a coordinated effort. The manufacturers, the distribution centers, they're all in on it. They're leaving breadcrumbs for those of us who are paying attention.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: In on what, exactly?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's what I'm trying to tell you! It's too big. If I say it out loud, they'll know I know.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'Oh no, I'd put one of your signs on my lawn if I wasn't worried about winning.'
jokes.setup: Someone offers to put Moira's sign on their lawn; Jocelyn says she would too
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's caveat — she'd support Moira except for the inconvenient fact that she's the opposing candidate who wants to win — is delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's blithe acknowledgment of self-interest while framing it as generosity is a perfect small character beat. The line is tighter than it looks — it does a lot in one sentence.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're using the same argument twice.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, I'm not. The first one was about the... um... habeas corpus, and the second one was about the... double jeopardy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Those are the exact same argument.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I'm very confident in both of them, so...

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh no, you mustn't beat yourself up. You have your own signs to worry about. All two hundred of them.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn says she doesn't stand for dirty politics
jokes.punchline: Moira's seemingly supportive response pivots to a pointed reminder of Jocelyn's sign advantage — 'all two hundred of them' — delivered as if it's afterthought comfort
jokes.explanation: Moira's passive-aggressive comfort — building up Jocelyn while subtly jabbing at her sign advantage — is executed with perfect cadence. 'All two hundred of them' as a trailing addendum is a well-placed button. Classic Moira: kindness weaponized.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You can't have 'catering' as a separate line item from 'meals.' They're the same thing.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Sing ladies... if you can.'
jokes.setup: The choir leader has suggested they focus on singing and not the political drama
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'if you can' undercuts the peace-making entirely — a barely-veiled insult to the choir's abilities wrapped in a directive to move on
jokes.explanation: Three words after a peace-making speech. 'If you can' is a perfect Moira addendum — she can't help herself, even in a conciliatory moment. Economy is near-perfect (9.5): four words carry condescension, competition, and character.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, did you take down your own real estate signs?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I prefer to think of it as a calculated strategic withdrawal. It's a sophisticated tactic used by the most discerning real estate professionals.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You took down your own signs.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, scarcity breeds desire. When clients can't find the property, they want it more.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Okay, well, I'm seeing a lot of women's tops, so if there's a minimum amount for free shipping can let me know before you order?'
jokes.setup: David is defensive about being caught browsing women's tops
jokes.punchline: Alexis doesn't judge — she just wants to know if she can piggyback on his order for free shipping, treating his transgression as a logistical opportunity
jokes.explanation: Alexis's complete non-reaction to David shopping women's tops — pivoting immediately to free shipping logistics — is excellent. It's funnier than any judgment would be: she accepts his behavior as given and just wants to benefit from it. Strong character integration (very Alexis) and the misdirection from expected judgment to practical request is well-constructed. Quotable and shareable. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it's a very simple strategy. I create a scandal, deny it vehemently, then I come back with an even bigger scandal that makes everyone forget about the first one. It's foolproof.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I once played a woman on a soap opera who stole her own baby to frame her sister. By the time anyone figured out it was her baby, she'd already burned down the family mansion, so nobody cared about the baby anymore.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I'm sorry. Were you picked up by the South Korean Secret Police on New Year's? I had to sweet talk the consulate's lawyer to get me a passport before midnight.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is questioning whether David can handle pressure in the meeting
jokes.punchline: Alexis's credentials for being 'good under pressure' is that she was detained by South Korean Secret Police and had to charm her way out by midnight — delivered as a casual résumé line
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's standout joke. Alexis's past life of international glamour-chaos is a running background element of the show, and the South Korean Secret Police on New Year's is specific enough to feel real (not generic 'I've been in dangerous situations'). The midnight consulate charm is an absurdist Cinderella story. The delivery is pure Alexis — completely casual about something most people would find traumatic. Strong originality and character integration: no other character on television could deliver this line. Justifies 8.3 craft and 8.5 impact.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A soap opera? That's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it should work like a soap opera. If I don't like what's happening, I should be able to just not show up for six months and have everything reset when I come back.

jokes.text: David is asked if he had a Cosmo Girl subscription at 16 — a long beat, then: 'No.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is testing David's ability to lie under pressure
jokes.punchline: The obvious pause before 'No' tells us everything — of course he had one, and his denial is the joke
jokes.explanation: The beat before 'No' is where the comedy lives — the audience knows the truth, David knows they know, and his flat denial is funnier than any admission. 'Cosmo Girl' as the specific magazine is excellent: specific enough to be both plausible and funny for teenage David. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: How could you accuse Roland of such a thing? Do you have any idea how that makes us look? We are not sign thieves, Johnny. We are many things—eccentric, yes; financially ruined, certainly—but we are not common criminals.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Okay, David, if you have to lie about something you should breathe out while you answer, 'cause it really just calms you down.'
jokes.setup: David has just clearly and badly lied about the Cosmo Girl subscription
jokes.punchline: Alexis offers lying technique advice as if it's a normal life skill — the implication being she's a practiced liar who has optimized the process
jokes.explanation: Alexis coaching David on lying technique — with a specific tip (breathe out) — is excellent character comedy. It tells us she has lied her way out of situations enough to have developed a method. The normality of the advice is what makes it funny. Very Alexis: practical, amoral, helpful.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, are those shoes Ferragamo? Because if you're wearing Ferragamo, you can definitely afford better lawyers.

jokes.text: David: 'Oh my God! I'm not being interrogated. This is a negotiation.' / Alexis: 'I'm just trying to prepare you, okay? 'Cause they're gonna eat you alive in there.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is stress-testing David before his meeting
jokes.punchline: David's outrage at the 'interrogation' while Alexis calmly insists they'll 'eat you alive' plays the siblings' dynamic perfectly — her confidence in his failure is the joke
jokes.explanation: A functional sibling-dynamic beat. Alexis's certainty that David will fail is both affectionate and cutting. Good but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's marginally better than 'Rose Apothecary: A Place Where Dreams Go to Die.'

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I know that we just met, but if Prince Harry trusted me with his life, then I think you can too.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to convince Wendy to trust her in the meeting
jokes.punchline: Her credential is that Prince Harry trusted her with his life — another of Alexis's casually-dropped outrageous past encounters
jokes.explanation: Prince Harry as a credentialing reference — delivered with complete earnestness — is very Alexis. The '...with his life' extension makes it more than just a name-drop; it implies a specific dangerous situation she can't or won't detail. Highly quotable. The pattern of Alexis's outrageous past is a strong running gag throughout the series, and this is a strong entry.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So you're telling me I can't use my own name? That's literally insane.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The trademark was filed in 1987. You were born in 1986.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well what am I supposed to call myself? Wendy?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's actually worse.

jokes.text: Wendy: 'Well, I do feel better having a lawyer in the room.' / Alexis: 'Oh my God, you're so cute. Um, I'm not a lawyer, but I'm glad that my fashion pony is working.'
jokes.setup: Wendy assumes Alexis is a lawyer based on her outfit
jokes.punchline: Alexis confirms she's NOT a lawyer — she just dressed to project authority, and she's pleased it's working. 'Fashion pony' as her term for the power-outfit is perfectly Alexis.
jokes.explanation: 'Fashion pony' is an inspired coinage — it's the right amount of Alexis-specific weirdness. The reveal that she's not a lawyer while delighting in having fooled someone is exactly her character. 'You're so cute' as condescension to an adult is also very Alexis. Justifies 8+: the phrase 'fashion pony' alone earns originality points.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We have another meeting to get to.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We're already in our store.

jokes.text: Oh, Mr. Rose! / Oh, hi Stevie.
jokes.setup: Scene transition — John is somewhere unexpected when Stevie appears
jokes.punchline: The overly casual mutual greeting suggests an awkward encounter, with John's pleasant 'Oh, hi Stevie' implying he's been caught somewhere or doing something
jokes.explanation: A setup beat more than a joke in itself — the comedy here depends entirely on what follows. The greeting alone is a mild awkward beat.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, Roland says the town council approved all of his signs, but between you and me, Roland may have donated to their campaign.

jokes.text: Since she's been so worried about her signs having been mysteriously stolen.
jokes.setup: Stevie is explaining why she's returning the signs
jokes.punchline: The word 'mysteriously' is doing quiet heavy lifting — Stevie's dry delivery implies she suspects it's not so mysterious, gently signaling to John that she knows what's going on
jokes.explanation: Stevie's 'mysteriously' is a great dry comedy word choice — it says everything without saying anything. Classic Stevie deadpan, and it implicitly judges both Moira (who staged the theft) and John (who is covering for her). Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: The campaign stress has really gotten to me. I said 'effing' yesterday.

jokes.text: John's increasingly desperate spin: 'She accidentally, uh, threw them out.' / Stevie: 'That's one theory.' / John: 'Or... someone stole them and hid them in our garbage... to make it look like she's done something... really crazy.'
jokes.setup: Stevie has found Moira's signs in their garbage; John is trying to explain it
jokes.punchline: John's improvised cover-up escalates from 'she threw them out' to an elaborate conspiracy theory that actually implicates Moira more than protects her — each sentence digs deeper
jokes.explanation: John's escalating improvised explanations — each one less plausible and more incriminating — is excellent comedy structure. The second theory (someone stole them to frame Moira as 'really crazy') is unintentionally more damning than the first. Stevie's dry 'That's one theory' is perfect straight-man work.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I've been having some stress symptoms lately. My doctor says I need to watch less television, but honestly, I think it's just one show in particular that's really affecting me.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: It's The Great British Bake Off. I get so emotionally invested in these bakers, and when someone gets eliminated, I just fall apart.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'That is definitely some food for thought.'
jokes.setup: John has just proposed that someone framed Moira by hiding signs in her own garbage
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat, polite 'That is definitely some food for thought' is a masterwork of deadpan — she clearly doesn't believe a word of it but responds as if it's a reasonable hypothesis
jokes.explanation: Stevie's deadpan is one of the show's great comic assets, and 'That is definitely some food for thought' is an excellent deployment. It's perfectly calibrated to communicate disbelief through total sincerity. The word 'definitely' amplifies the irony. Economy is near-perfect. Justifies 8+: this is professional-grade deadpan writing, Stevie-specific, and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I've been having heart palpitations, insomnia, and I cried during a children's cooking show yesterday.

jokes.text: The lawyers open by presenting a check with zero discussion: 'This shouldn't take long. The check.'
jokes.setup: David and Alexis have prepared for a serious negotiation
jokes.punchline: The lawyers immediately flash a check — expecting this to be a done deal — not knowing they're about to be outmaneuvered by a reality TV-educated non-lawyer in borrowed clothes
jokes.explanation: Setup for the negotiation scene — the lawyers' overconfidence is the context for all the comedy that follows. Functional rather than exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose I've won by default. How deliciously anticlimactic. I had such elaborate plans for my victory speech.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I just can't do this anymore, Moira. I'm dropping out.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, darling, you're giving up? After I've spent weeks perfecting my acceptance remarks and securing a tiara rental?

jokes.text: Alexis objects to the lawyer's itemized costs: 'Objection sustained.'
jokes.setup: David has just pointed out that the lawyer mentioned 'signs and business cards' again after Wendy already listed them
jokes.punchline: Alexis says 'Objection sustained' — as if she is the judge sustaining her own objection, conflating the roles in a way that reveals she has no legal training whatsoever
jokes.explanation: Alexis sustaining her own objection — combining the roles of lawyer and judge — is a great absurdist legal joke. Two words reveal her entire understanding of courtroom procedure. Economy is excellent. The specific legal confusion (objection vs. sustain) shows a real knowledge of legal vocabulary deployed incorrectly. Highly quotable. Justifies 8+ — it's specific, character-perfect, and the wrong usage is exactly right.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to stop you right there. When you say 'big check,' I'm assuming you mean an actual check, and not one of those oversized novelty checks that people inexplicably find charming.

jokes.text: David: 'Which is very similar to the meals and not necessarily relevant, so...' — about catering
jokes.setup: Alexis is inflating Wendy's costs to counter the lawyers, including 'catering'
jokes.punchline: David, playing along but slightly embarrassed, parenthetically notes that catering and meals overlap — showing his brand-manager logic trying to maintain credibility in a charade
jokes.explanation: A nice sibling-dynamic moment — David is trying to rein in Alexis's overreach while still playing ball. His half-hearted correction is character-consistent.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You could accomplish a lot in four years on the town council.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Four years?!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't even commit to a skincare routine for four years.

jokes.text: There is the travel, there are the meals, there is the shipping and the catering... / Which is very similar to the meals and not necessarily relevant, so...
jokes.setup: Alexis is padding the cost list with increasingly creative items
jokes.punchline: Even Lisa can't help pointing out that 'catering' is 'very similar to the meals' — a bureaucratic observation that undercuts the whole negotiation while remaining politely indirect
jokes.explanation: The joke of someone padding a claim with obviously duplicative items (meals AND catering) and being caught at it by the other side is a good structure. Lisa's precise, polite objection is funny in its understatement.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what? Save? I don't understand what you're asking me to do with this word.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'And let's be honest, Blouse House doesn't have quite the same ring to it.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is making the argument that the Australian company truly needs this specific name
jokes.punchline: She invokes 'Blouse House' — the alternate name Wendy nearly chose — as the threat of what they'd be left with, turning it into leverage
jokes.explanation: Calling back 'Blouse House' as negotiating leverage is a neat callback that shows Alexis was paying attention. The line works because 'Blouse House' is genuinely terrible, so the leverage is real even if Alexis is bluffing about everything else.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I do my own nails.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I do my own nails. There, I said it.

jokes.text: Well, the $10,000 doesn't even cover our legal fees, so...
jokes.setup: The Australians are pushing back on the expanding list of costs
jokes.punchline: David mentions 'our legal fees' — for a lawyer who isn't a lawyer — delivered with complete confidence
jokes.explanation: The audacity of billing for legal fees when you're not a lawyer is a good escalation of the con. Works in context of the running Alexis-as-lawyer joke.

jokes.text: And if we're gonna announce a new name, there has to be a whole relaunch event, and that involves... Event planners and, um, florists.
jokes.setup: Alexis is continuing to invent costs to inflate the settlement
jokes.punchline: The trailing 'um' before 'Event planners' and then the addition of 'florists' reveals Alexis is improvising in real time, adding whatever occurs to her
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'event planners' and addition of 'florists' signals Alexis improvising, which is funny because it reveals the con in progress. Works better in performance than on the page.

jokes.text: The singing rehearsal interruption: 'No! No, no, Gwen, you took the lower third. Well, could you please then? I've already locked mine in.'
jokes.setup: In the middle of a tense negotiation scene cut, we jump to the choir rehearsal
jokes.punchline: The mundane chaos of choir harmony disputes — 'I've already locked mine in' — as a counterpoint to the high-stakes Blouse Barn negotiation is a tonal joke
jokes.explanation: A transitional moment — the choir dispute is lightly funny but isn't a standout joke. Functions as scene-break comedy more than a standalone bit.

jokes.text: Moira's soap opera logic: 'Politics 101, John. When you have limited resources, your best course of action is to create a stir. It's like that episode of Sunrise Bay when I stole my own baby.'
jokes.setup: Moira is explaining to John why she stole her own campaign signs
jokes.punchline: Her political strategy is directly inspired by a soap opera plot in which she stole her own baby — delivered with complete authority as if this is a valid precedent
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. Moira citing a soap opera plot — one in which SHE stole her own baby — as a legitimate political strategy model is perfectly her. Every element is right: the authority ('Politics 101'), the Sunrise Bay callback, the casual mention of baby theft, and John's incredulous 'Moira, Sunrise Bay was a soap opera.' / 'Yes, and this is reality.' The exchange that follows ('building a mystery' vs. 'misleading people') elevates it further. Character integration is a 10: only Moira could say this, in this way, with this certainty. Quotable, shocking, and completely earned. Top-tier Schitt's Creek writing.

jokes.text: John: 'Moira, Sunrise Bay was a soap opera.' / Moira: 'Yes, and this is reality.'
jokes.setup: Moira has compared her political strategy to stealing her own baby on a soap opera
jokes.punchline: John points out it was fiction; Moira affirms 'yes, and this is reality' — the juxtaposition of the baby-theft strategy with 'reality' makes the distinction meaningless
jokes.explanation: The payoff line of joke #53. 'Yes, and this is reality' functions as: (a) an acknowledgment, (b) an assertion that reality and her soap opera are comparable, and (c) a complete non-sequitur defense. The economy is exceptional — four words that deepen rather than explain. Works in tandem with #53 as a two-part bit. Scored as a separate entry because it stands on its own as a punchline.

jokes.text: Moira: 'No, I'm building a mystery. Voters will draw their own conclusions.'
jokes.setup: John accuses Moira of misleading people
jokes.punchline: Moira reframes her deception as 'building a mystery' — a media-trained, PR-polished non-answer that's technically accurate and completely absurd
jokes.explanation: 'Building a mystery' is a brilliant Moira-ism — it's the spin-doctor reframe of 'I lied' into something glamorous. The fact that she genuinely believes this framing is the joke. Highly quotable as a standalone phrase. Character integration is strong: this is exactly how Moira processes her own behavior.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Well, then hopefully you've learned something — you shouldn't make accusations you can't substantiate.'
jokes.setup: John says he essentially accused Roland of stealing the signs
jokes.punchline: Moira, who just admitted to faking the theft herself, lectures John about making unsubstantiated accusations — the hypocrisy is total and delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: The full-circle hypocrisy — Moira, who engineered the situation that caused John to accuse Roland, now lecturing him about evidence-based accusations — is executed with perfect sincerity. Moira's ability to be wrong and righteous simultaneously is a defining character trait and this is a prime example. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I'm not listening until you calm down.'
jokes.setup: John is (reasonably) frustrated with Moira's ethical rationalizations
jokes.punchline: Moira deflects John's legitimate anger by demanding he calm down first — a classic deflection move delivered with regal serenity
jokes.explanation: A familiar move elevated by Moira's specific application of it. The serene delivery of 'I'm not listening until you calm down' is funny because she is the chaos and he is (relatively) calm. Character-specific but not exceptional.

jokes.text: David: 'Well, the point is, judging by your bag and those shoes, Blouse Barn Australia is paying you quite a bit of money to come here and help us switch over our signs.'
jokes.setup: The lawyer asks the 'point' of the $100 million revenue figure David cited
jokes.punchline: David pivots from business facts to fashion assessment — using the lawyer's accessories as evidence of her firm's wealth, which is both insightful and absurdly David
jokes.explanation: David using fashion forensics as negotiating evidence is exactly his character — he would notice the bag and shoes, and he would use them. The pivot from revenue figures to accessories is a great misdirection that also proves his point. Very David.

jokes.text: David slides a number across the table — we don't see the figure — and says 'Now, if you'll excuse us, we have another meeting to get to.' / Wendy: 'We're in our store.'
jokes.setup: David is playing hardball negotiator, doing the power move of sliding a number and walking out
jokes.punchline: Wendy immediately undercuts the power move by pointing out they're literally in their own store — there's nowhere to walk out to
jokes.explanation: The contrast between David's high-power negotiation theatre and the actual context ('we're in our store') is perfectly constructed. 'We're in our store' as a complete deflation of the power-walk is economical and situationally funny. Wendy's oblivious sincerity makes it land harder. Justifies 8+: the setup (negotiation cliché) and punchline (physical reality) are excellently matched.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'I know. Roland got carried away and used his mayoral power to get people to put more signs on their lawns. And I just... pretended it wasn't happening.'
jokes.setup: Moira and Jocelyn are having a frank 'campaign ethics' discussion
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's admission that Roland abused his mayoral authority for lawn signs — and she just ignored it — is both an admission of complicity and a perfectly Jocelyn response (passive, optimistic non-confrontation)
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's 'I just pretended it wasn't happening' is a great character beat — her conflict-avoidance as a survival strategy with Roland is both funny and sympathetic.

jokes.text: Moira reframes Roland's coercion: 'Roland forced constituents to put up signs through coercion? That's what we're talking about, right?' — while nodding to signal Jocelyn to agree
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has euphemistically described Roland's sign-pushing as him getting 'carried away'
jokes.punchline: Moira re-states it in the most politically damning terms possible, then asks Jocelyn to confirm — essentially coaching Jocelyn to help Moira build a case against Roland
jokes.explanation: Moira's strategic reframing — taking Jocelyn's soft admission and converting it to 'coercion' — with the confirmatory 'right?' is excellent character comedy. She's simultaneously extracting a confession and directing the scene.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'The other day, I called another driver an effing moron. I didn't say the f word, but I said effing, and that is not me!'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is explaining the campaign has changed her — driven her to uncharacteristic behavior
jokes.punchline: The shocking behavior is... saying 'effing' instead of 'eff.' Her horror at her own (barely) elevated language reveals the extreme of her gentle baseline
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The setup is that the campaign has driven Jocelyn to behave out of character; the reveal is that 'out of character' means saying 'effing' instead of literally nothing. The specific distinction — 'I didn't say the f word, but I said effing' — is perfect character comedy. Jocelyn's horror at this minimal transgression is funnier than any actual swearing would be. High originality: the specificity of the distinction (effing vs. nothing) is unexpected. Justifies 8.3+ craft score.

jokes.text: The other day, I called another driver an effing moron. I didn't say the 'f' word, but I said, 'effing,' and that is not me!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn explains why she's dropping out — the campaign is changing her
jokes.punchline: Her evidence of moral corruption: she said 'effing' — not even the actual word — and is traumatized by this level of profanity
jokes.explanation: A standout character joke. Jocelyn's moral threshold being 'effing' — not even the actual word — is perfectly calibrated for her character. 'That is not me!' delivered as a genuine crisis of identity is excellent. The specificity (she specifies she said 'effing,' not the f-word, as though this distinction matters enormously) makes it. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I don't think it's exactly unethical to do questionable things in the spirit of competition.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is feeling guilty about Roland's sign tactics
jokes.punchline: Moira's philosophical justification for ethical violations — 'not exactly unethical to do questionable things' — is a masterpiece of self-serving logic
jokes.explanation: 'Not exactly unethical to do questionable things' is a beautifully constructed contradiction — it almost sounds like a logical statement until you parse it. Moira delivering this as reassurance to Jocelyn while she's the primary offender is rich irony. The phrasing is Moira-specific and quotable. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Jocelyn drops out because she 'can't get through an episode of Masterchef Junior without crying'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is cataloguing how the campaign has affected her mental state
jokes.punchline: Among the symptoms of her campaign-induced breakdown: inability to watch Masterchef Junior without crying — the specific show choice (children cooking competition) as an emotional trigger is the joke
jokes.explanation: Masterchef Junior as the breaking point is an inspired specific choice — it's sweet enough that crying is understandable, but it's also fundamentally low-stakes enough to be funny as a breakdown trigger. Very Jocelyn: it's wholesome chaos. Quotable and specific.

jokes.text: Wendy presents a 'big check' to David. David: 'It's a big check!' — beat — 'Oh... Um, you don't mean one of those big lottery checks, right?'
jokes.setup: Wendy says 'It's a big check, David' with evident excitement
jokes.punchline: David mishears/misunderstands 'big' as referring to a novelty oversized lottery check, not a large amount — the misread is funny both as wordplay and as David-specific anxiety about what 'big' means in their reduced circumstances
jokes.explanation: A nice wordplay moment — 'big check' triggering the lottery check interpretation — with added comedy from David's obvious relief at clarification. The beat between 'Oh...' and the lottery question is good comic timing.

jokes.text: Wendy: 'I wouldn't go that far.' — in response to David saying 'thank you for trusting me'
jokes.setup: David thanks Wendy for trusting him
jokes.punchline: Wendy immediately clarifies she wouldn't characterize it as trust — she just didn't stop him
jokes.explanation: A quick dry line that works on economy alone. Not exceptional but delivers a small laugh.

jokes.text: John and Moira reunite: Moira is delighted to be on council. John says 'that's the spirit, positive thinking.' Moira: 'I won! Jocelyn quit the election.'
jokes.setup: John sees Moira excited about being on council and assumes she's being optimistic
jokes.punchline: She actually won — because her opponent quit — and she reports it with the same triumphant energy she'd have if she'd won on merit
jokes.explanation: The misdirection — John thinking she's being hopeful, then the reveal she actually won by default — is a solid structural joke. Moira's equal delight at a default win as she'd have at a real one is the character note.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Four years? No one said anything about four years.'
jokes.setup: John congratulates Moira on what she can accomplish in four years as councillor
jokes.punchline: Moira hadn't considered the term length — she wanted the win but not the job, and the duration genuinely surprises her
jokes.explanation: Moira's horror at learning she's won a four-year commitment is an excellent reversal — she campaigned for the title without considering the responsibilities. The specificity of 'four years' as a revelation is funny. Very Moira: interested in the crown, not the governance.

jokes.text: Moira: 'And the thought of working with Roland every day. I mean, that's...' / John: 'I wouldn't even think about that.'
jokes.setup: Moira is processing the full horror of what she's won
jokes.punchline: The thought of Roland as a daily colleague is apparently worse than the four-year commitment — and John's 'I wouldn't even think about that' confirms it while offering no comfort
jokes.explanation: John's advice to simply not think about Roland as a coping mechanism is both practical and useless — a warm John moment where the comfort is worse than no comfort.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh, John. What have I done?'
jokes.setup: Moira has just processed the full consequences of winning: four years, Roland as a colleague
jokes.punchline: The dramatic 'What have I done?' over winning a small-town council seat is perfectly pitched Moira self-drama — proportionally enormous reaction to a proportionally small event
jokes.explanation: A perfect Moira button on her arc this episode — the existential 'What have I done?' for a council seat she engineered to win. The proportionality is the joke. Good economy (four words), strong character integration.

jokes.text: Oh, John. What have I done?
jokes.setup: The realization of what council membership entails hits Moira
jokes.punchline: Moira's theatrical despair — 'What have I done?' — treats winning a small-town council seat as a tragic fate, the ultimate irony of her entire campaign arc
jokes.explanation: A perfect closing beat for Moira's arc in the episode. The irony is complete: she ran for council using soap opera tactics, won by accident, and now faces the reality of governance as a tragedy. 'What have I done?' is a line usually reserved for catastrophes, applied here to winning a local election.

jokes.text: David's reaction to $40,000: 'Divided? I'm the one who negotiated all of this.'
jokes.setup: The family celebrates the $40,000 windfall; someone suggests dividing it four ways
jokes.punchline: David immediately objects to division — his self-credit for the negotiation is both somewhat true and completely ignoring that Alexis was there too
jokes.explanation: David's possessiveness over the negotiation victory is consistent character behavior. Works but doesn't stand out.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Um, with my help, David.' / David: 'I believe I offered advice.' / Alexis: 'You said Wendy should cave and take the first offer. What you gave was the opposite of advice.'
jokes.setup: David is claiming sole credit; Alexis is correcting the record
jokes.punchline: Alexis's takedown — 'what you gave was the opposite of advice' — is precise and completely accurate, a callback to John's earlier useless advice and David's own early position
jokes.explanation: 'The opposite of advice' is a clean, sharp put-down — a callback to David's earlier position that the store should just take the money. Alexis earns her credit through accurate recollection. Efficient sibling comedy.

jokes.text: David's attempt to say 'save': 'Shouldn't we be s... s-s-s... Should we be... Trying to sa... save this? Isn't... Shouldn't we be saving... Putting this in a bank? Isn't that what people do?'
jokes.setup: The family is excitedly planning to spend the $40,000
jokes.punchline: David — whose entire identity has been wealth and spending — physically cannot bring himself to say the word 'save' without stuttering multiple attempts. The extended, halting attempt is the joke, with 'isn't that what people do?' as the button
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's finest moments — a sustained performance joke where David's psychological inability to suggest financial responsibility manifests as a literal verbal inability to say 'save.' The escalation of failed attempts, followed by the existential 'Isn't that what people do?' as if 'saving' is a foreign concept he's heard of but never practiced, is perfectly constructed. It rewards the character's entire arc and the Rose family's background. Character integration is nearly perfect (9.5): no other character in TV comedy has this specific crisis. The escalating structure (s... s-s-s... sa...) builds beautifully. Justifies 8.6 craft and 9.0 impact — this is among the episode's two or three best moments.

jokes.text: John: 'Well, that's no fun. No... but probably best?'
jokes.setup: David has just managed to suggest they save the money
jokes.punchline: John — equally constitutionally opposed to saving — admits it's probably best while clearly hating the idea, mirroring David's conflict
jokes.explanation: John's reluctant endorsement of saving — 'No... but probably best?' — mirrors David's struggle and extends the family-wide aversion to financial responsibility. The question mark at the end of 'probably best' is the joke.

jokes.text: The family pivots from saving to manicures: 'Maybe we could do manicures. I could [get] a manicure. The three of us should go.' / David: 'Wait a minute, just 'cause I do my own nails doesn't mean, you know, I wouldn't go...'
jokes.setup: They've just agreed to save the $40,000
jokes.punchline: Within seconds they've agreed on manicures as a 'little treat' — and David is defensive about doing his own nails as a reason he wouldn't appreciate manicures, while also clearly wanting to go
jokes.explanation: The instant collapse from 'we should save' to 'manicures!' is a perfect callback button — the Roses cannot help themselves. David's detail ('I do my own nails') adds texture and the defensive pre-justification for wanting to join is very David. Warm family comedy that earns its resolution.

jokes.text: Alexis: ''Kay, they're just expensive and you wouldn't really appreciate them.' / David: 'Oh yay, David! Yay!'
jokes.setup: David is about to accept the manicure invite
jokes.punchline: Alexis disinvites him ('you wouldn't really appreciate them'), so David sarcastically cheers himself for being excluded — the petulance is the joke
jokes.explanation: David sarcastically cheering his own exclusion — 'Oh yay, David! Yay!' — is the episode's closing gag and a perfect cap on his arc. The self-directed sarcasm and the fact that he wanted to go makes it funnier. Very David. Strong closing beat.

tmdb_overview: Moira’s campaign takes a turn when some of her lawn signs mysteriously disappear. David and Alexis must work together to get David’s boss out of legal trouble.
insight: David's real-estate ambitions clash with Rose family chaos across 55 jokes.
